column1: # OWASP ZAP baseline rule overrides — clinipharma.com.br
# Format: <plugin_id>\t<action>\t<comment>
#   action ∈ { IGNORE, WARN, FAIL }
# IGNORE means the rule is silenced everywhere. Prefer WARN over IGNORE
# whenever we want the finding visible (in the HTML report) but not
# blocking the build.
# Every IGNORE here MUST justify why we accept the finding — drift in
# the rationale is a code-review smell. Re-evaluate annually.
# Reference: https://www.zaproxy.org/docs/alerts/
# We protect mutating endpoints with the Origin/Referer + double-submit
# cookie pattern (lib/security/csrf.ts), not synchroniser tokens. ZAP's
# heuristic does not recognise this pattern, so the alert fires on
# every form. Documented in docs/security/dynamic-scanning.md.
# ─── Storable but non-cacheable content (10049) ──────────────────────
# Next.js streams server components without `Cache-Control: no-store`
# on intentionally public pages (/, /login, /trust). The framework's
# default is correct for our threat model — sensitive responses already
# carry `Cache-Control: private, no-store` via dedicated handlers.

column3: (Streaming RSC default; sensitive routes set their own no-store)

column1: # ─── Cookie without SameSite attribute (10054) ───────────────────────
# Supabase SSR sets short-lived auth cookies WITHOUT SameSite at the
# JWT-cookie layer (the host-level SameSite=Lax default applies). The
# CSRF mitigation lives one layer higher (Origin + double-submit), so
# this finding is not a security gap for our model. Tracked: revisit
# when @supabase/ssr exposes a SameSite=Strict opt-in.

column3: (Supabase SSR cookies — SameSite via browser default; CSRF via Origin+double-submit)

column1: # Already enforced via next.config.ts securityHeaders. ZAP sometimes
# misses it on streaming responses where the header arrives in the
# initial chunk. Keep as WARN so a real regression still surfaces.

column3: (Header set in next.config.ts; streaming may delay first-chunk inspection)
